input_article.title: New US visa rules from March 30 expand screening for H-1B applicants
input_article.summary: The US State Department has announced that it will expand its screening process for visa applicants to include a broader review of their online presence, starting from March 30. The new rules will apply to a wide range of non-immigrant visa categories, including H-1B visas and family-based visas such as fiancé(e) and spouse categories. The expanded review will also cover cultural and religious visas, as well as special categories such as informants and victims of crime or trafficking.

results.story_summary: The US Department of State announced that it will expand its screening process for visa applicants starting March 30, requiring a broader review of their online presence and mandating public access to social media profiles. The new rules will apply to a wider range of non-immigrant visa categories, including H-1B visas, H-4 dependents, and family-based visas such as K visas. Applicants are instructed to adjust the privacy settings on all of their social media profiles to "public" to facilitate this vetting. The move aims to address abuse of the H-1B program while permitting companies to hire the best temporary foreign workers.

The UDF coalition is led by Congress and is contesting the upcoming Kerala Assembly elections on issues such as anti-incumbency against the ruling LDF government and criticism of the CPI(M)-BJP alliance. Satheesan claims that the BJP's vote share decreased in the 2021 elections due to internal infighting, with some party leaders being sidelined or handed over to smaller allies.

The UDF campaign strategy is seen as more unified than in previous elections, with Satheesan promising a "team" approach among allies. The BJP-led National Democratic Alliance (NDA) is also expected to make a mark in the polls.

input_article.title: Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at Kennedy Space Center as Nasa eyes April 1 Moon launch
input_article.summary: The crew of NASA's Artemis 2 mission has arrived at the Kennedy Space Center, marking the final phase of preparations before liftoff on April 1. The astronauts will test major systems on board Orion during the 10-day mission, which will include life support systems, navigation systems, and communication systems. The Artemis 2 mission is expected to be the first crewed journey around the Moon since the Apollo 17 mission in 1972, with a free return trajectory that leads back to Earth.

results.story_summary: The crew of NASA's Artemis 2 mission has arrived at Kennedy Space Center, marking a major step towards its historic launch on April 1. The four astronauts will spend about 10 days testing Orion spacecraft systems before flying by the Moon and returning to Earth. After overcoming technical issues, including a liquid hydrogen leak and helium flow problems, the mission is now on track for its ambitious goal of humanity's return to the lunar surface. NASA's commitment to safety remains top priority as it prepares for this milestone launch in early 2026.

input_article.title: Why AI systems don’t learn on their own: New research proposes a human-like solution
input_article.summary: Researchers have identified two fundamental learning modes in animals and humans: System A, which learns from observation, and System B, which learns through action. Current AI systems lack this dual learning approach, requiring extensive human intervention to retrain them when conditions change. Researchers propose adding a new system, System M (Meta-Control), that can dynamically manage learning and make meta-decisions based on internal signals and task performance.

results.story_summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) models face a fundamental limitation: they require constant retraining as conditions change, unlike humans who continuously adapt through exploration and experience. Researchers propose a radical solution by integrating two learning modes - System A (learning from observation) and System B (learning from action) - to enable AI systems to better adapt to real-world situations. This approach could lead to more robust AI models that can learn from their own mistakes and perform unpredictably in changing environments. The solution is based on insights from cognitive science and was published in a new paper by Emmanuel Dupoux, Yann LeCun, and Jitendra Malik.

input_article.title: Rare footage captures sperm whale birth in wild; other females assist in ‘first-of-its-kind’ behaviour: Watch
input_article.summary: Scientists from Project CETI captured rare footage of a sperm whale giving birth in the wild near Dominica in July 2023. The event was witnessed by the mother's pod, including females unrelated to her, who positioned themselves beneath the mother and assisted during childbirth. The calf was born after approximately five hours of labor and was later spotted again swimming with other juveniles in the group.

results.story_summary:
Here is a 3-4 line summary of the news:

Scientists from Project CETI have captured rare footage of a sperm whale giving birth in the wild, marking one of the few documented events in marine research. The event, observed off the Caribbean island of Dominica, saw a group of female whales gather to assist both the mother and her calf, providing unprecedented insight into cetacean behavior. This remarkable instance is believed to be the first recorded evidence of birth assistance in non-primates. The footage was published in Scientific Reports and Science, shedding new light on the complex social dynamics of sperm whale societies.

input_article.title: A 770-km race against time: How a ‘jump-start’ miracle brought the first Great Indian Bustard chick to Gujarat in a decade
input_article.summary: A Great Indian Bustard (GIB) chick was born in Gujarat on March 26 after a "trans-state conservation effort" that involved transporting a 15-day-old fertile egg from Rajasthan to Kutch. The egg was incubated by a female GIB and hatched on March 26, marking the country's first birth of the critically endangered bird under the "jumpstart" method. This is a significant development for the GIB conservation programme, which aims to recover the species that has only three surviving females in the wild in Gujarat.

results.story_summary:
Here's a unified news summary combining all sources:

Gujarat has witnessed its first Great Indian Bustard (GIB) chick birth in over a decade, thanks to a novel conservation effort called the "jumpstart" method. A captive-bred GIB egg from Rajasthan was transported 770km by road without breaks and replaced in a nest on March 22, hatching into a healthy chick after incubation. The achievement marks a significant development for the species' conservation program, as only three female GIBs remain in Gujarat's Kutch grasslands, making natural breeding impossible.

The successful trans-state conservation effort marks the first use of "jump-start" approach, which involves replacing an infertile egg with a fertile one to raise chicks in the wild, reducing egg predation risk.

input_article.title: Ancient ape fossils in Egypt desert may rewrite history of human ancestors
input_article.summary: Fossils of an ape-like creature, Masripithecus moghraensis, were discovered in northern Egypt and date back to between 17 million and 18 million years ago. The fossils, found during fieldwork in 2023 and 2024, are incomplete but provide new clues about early primate evolution. Scientists believe the species may have been a common ancestor of modern apes, including humans, chimpanzees, and gorillas.

results.story_summary: Scientists have reevaluated the origin of modern apes after discovering 17-18 million-year-old ape-like fossils in northern Egypt. The newly found fossils, belonging to a previously unknown species named Masripithecus moghraensis, suggest that the ancestors of all living apes, including humans, may have originated in northeast Africa or Arabia. This challenges the long-held assumption that modern apes evolved in East Africa.

input_article.title: Married persons can’t be in a live-in relationship without divorcing spouses: Allahabad HC
input_article.summary: A petition in India was rejected by a court, which stated that a married couple's freedom to live together without getting divorced is not absolute and must respect the other spouse's right. The court ruled that if one partner wants to live with someone else, they must obtain a divorce from their current spouse first. The decision also noted that while there is no law prohibiting consensual cohabitation for married couples, it does not necessarily protect them against prosecution under certain sections of the Indian Penal Code.
